tiers.name: Free Market
tiers.description: No Certificate of Need law. Healthcare providers can open facilities, expand services, and purchase equipment without government permission.

tiers.name: Mostly Free
tiers.description: CON has been largely repealed or reduced to a narrow scope. Minimal services still regulated, typically limited to nursing homes or specific equipment.

tiers.name: Moderate
tiers.description: CON law exists with meaningful scope but contains significant exemptions or has been partially reformed. Some services are deregulated.

tiers.name: Restrictive
tiers.description: Broad CON law covering most major healthcare services. Application process creates significant barriers to entry. Incumbent providers can intervene.

tiers.name: Highly Restrictive
tiers.description: Extensive CON law covering numerous services. High fees, long timelines, and aggressive incumbent intervention. Reform efforts have repeatedly failed.

tiers.name: Most Restrictive
tiers.description: Maximum CON restrictiveness. Nearly every healthcare service requires permission. Markets are highly concentrated. Reform is politically blocked.

dimensions: Breadth of regulated activities (new construction, expansion, equipment, relocation)
scoring_methodology.note: Scores reflect the statutory and regulatory burden of the CON program. A score of 0 means no CON law exists. A score of 100 means maximum restrictiveness across all dimensions.

dimensions.label: Bill Introduced
dimensions.description: A reform or repeal bill was introduced in the current or most recent legislative session.

dimensions.label: Committee Assignment
dimensions.description: The bill received a committee assignment, indicating it was taken seriously by leadership.

dimensions.label: Hearing Held
dimensions.description: The bill received a hearing, markup session, or floor vote.

dimensions.label: Governor Support
dimensions.description: The sitting governor has publicly supported CON reform or repeal.

dimensions.label: Prior Reform
dimensions.description: The state has previously reformed its CON law through partial repeal, exemptions, or scope reduction.
